patch.content:
## The Question Every Parent Asks

Today So Far exists to answer the question every parent asks a dozen times a day: "How’s the day going?" Not how did the last feed go. Not how long was the nap. The holistic question: taking everything together — feeding, sleep, activity, digestion, mood — is today a good day or a hard day?

The smart card renders a real-time summary of everything that has happened since midnight, designed to be understood in a single scroll on a phone screen.

## The Vertical Timeline

The card’s core is a chronological list of today’s events: feeds, sleeps, diaper changes, activities, medications, milestone observations. Each entry shows the time, a domain-colored icon, and a brief description. The list reads like a diary of the day, written in data rather than words.

A warmth gradient bar at the top gives an at-a-glance quality score. The gradient shifts from cool (blue-gray) through warm (amber) to vibrant (gold) based on the composite day score from the intelligence module. Parents learn to read the bar intuitively: a quick glance at the color tells them whether the day is tracking well before they read any details.

## The Horizontal Timeline Rejection

The original design proposed a horizontal scrolling timeline with events plotted along a time axis. This was rejected decisively (documented as rej-0001) because a 375px phone screen made horizontal scroll unusable for dense data. The timeline would show 3–4 hours at a time, requiring constant scrolling to see the full day. The vertical chronological list won on readability, scannability, and the simple fact that vertical scrolling is the natural gesture on a phone.

## Pattern-Based Nudges

The card doesn’t just report — it nudges. If Ziva hasn’t eaten in 3 hours and her usual interval is 2.5, a gentle prompt appears: "It’s been a while since the last feed." If her nap is running 30 minutes longer than her average, the card notes it neutrally: "Nap running long today." If the daily feeding volume is tracking below her weekly average, a soft flag appears.

These nudges are pattern-based, not rule-based. They learn from Ziva’s actual rhythms rather than imposing textbook schedules. A baby who naturally feeds every 3.5 hours doesn’t get nudged at 2.5 hours just because a chart says so. The system adapts to the child, not the other way around.

## The Spec Journey

The Today So Far spec went through 4 review rounds and reached 533 lines before it was declared build-ready. 12 functions carry the _tsf prefix. The card rebuilds on every data change via the onChange listener. It was one of the most heavily specified features in SproutLab history, because getting a daily summary wrong means parents distrust the whole app. If the card says "good day" when the day feels hard, or flags a concern when everything is fine, it loses credibility permanently.
